meta.title: Sprinter W901–905 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes Sprinter W901–905 engine — OM601, OM602, OM611, OM612, M111. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the OM601 legend explained. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes Sprinter W901–905 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned first‑generation Sprinter engine typically costs £1,500–£6,000 supply only [MB‑QUOTE], depending on engine — the OM601 2.3 diesel is the cheapest at £1,500–£3,500, while the OM612 2.7 CDI runs £3,500–£6,000. Fitting adds £780–£1,040. Use our calculator for a personalised quote.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes Sprinter W901–905 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM601 2.3‑litre naturally‑aspirated diesel (208 D, 308 D, 408 D) is legendary — it's a simple, indirect‑injection four‑cylinder with a reputation for exceeding 300,000 miles with basic maintenance. The OM602 2.9‑litre five‑cylinder turbo‑diesel is also robust and offers better torque for heavier payloads.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes Sprinter W901–905?

acceptedAnswer.text: Phase I (1995–2000) OM601/OM602 diesels can suffer injection pump wear; Phase II (2000–2006) OM611/OM612 CDI units have injector failure and EGR clogging issues; OM602 turbochargers can wear over time; and M111 petrol engines can develop timing chain stretch. Regular servicing is critical across all engines.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes Sprinter W901–905 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on the engine and era. The pre‑2000 OM601 and OM602 diesels are widely regarded as among the most durable diesel engines Mercedes ever built. The post‑2000 CDI units are more refined but introduced electronic complexity and injector wear. Overall, a well‑maintained first‑generation Sprinter can be exceptionally durable.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes Sprinter W901–905 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine is an outright "avoid" — but a high‑mileage OM611/OM612 CDI without documented injector history should be approached with caution. The OM601 is the safe bet for budget‑conscious buyers who value simplicity and durability over power.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my Sprinter W901–905 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Key warning signs include: hard starting or rough running (OM601/OM602 injection pump), rough idle or excessive smoke (OM611/OM612 injectors), loss of power or smoke (OM602 turbo), or rattling on start‑up (M111 timing chain). If you experience any of these, get a diagnosis immediately — early intervention saves thousands.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes Sprinter W901–905 engine, or should I scrap the van?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the van's value and condition. Against a well‑kept £6,000 Sprinter, a £3,500 OM601 recon is 58% of the van's value — safe if the van is otherwise sound. Against a tired £2,500 example, the same recon is 140% — walk away. Use our 70% rule as your guide.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes Sprinter W901–905 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: With proper maintenance, the OM601 diesel can exceed 300,000 miles, and the OM602 is similarly durable. The OM611/OM612 CDI units are capable of 200,000+ miles with regular injector maintenance. The M111 petrol can reach 200,000 miles with careful chain monitoring.

mainEntity.name: What do the W901–905 chassis codes mean?

acceptedAnswer.text: The W901, W902, W903, W904 and W905 codes denote the gross vehicle weight rating — from 2.8t up to 6.0t — not distinct body styles. All are first‑generation Sprinter (T1N) vans. The engine range is consistent across all weight variants.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes Sprinter W901–905 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate (visible on the door pillar) and in the vehicle documentation. The code is stamped on the engine block — on OM601/OM602 it's on the left side, on OM611/OM612 it's on the front left, and on M111 it's on the right side. Tell us your variant and registration year, and we'll identify it for you.

hero.h1: Mercedes Sprinter W901–905 Engines — The Complete UK Guide

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes Sprinter W901–905 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the first‑generation Sprinter (T1N) — spanning Phase I (1995–2000) and Phase II (2000–2006) with the introduction of CDI common‑rail technology.

confidenceScore.title: Confidence Score
confidenceScore.text: All ratings: Expert‑knowledge tier, derived from cross‑referencing specialist and owner sources — confirm against the Model Page before publishing. The OM601 and OM602 naturally‑aspirated/turbo units are widely regarded as among the most durable diesel engines Mercedes ever built. Data sources note: Enquiry volumes: EM_Proprietary_Data_2025.txt [MB‑VERIFIED]. Costs: Table A — Engine Replacement Economics [MB‑QUOTE]. Reliability ratings: provisional — no dedicated Engine Page exists for any of these families; ratings should be reconciled against the published Mercedes Sprinter Model Page.

overview.intro: The first‑generation Sprinter (internal designation T1N) launched in 1995, replacing the long‑running T1 van. Built on a ladder‑frame chassis with rear‑wheel drive, it was offered in a vast range of body styles — panel van, crew cab, chassis cab, minibus, and pickup — with gross vehicle weights from 2.8t to 6.0t denoted by the W901–905 codes. The engine range evolved significantly mid‑life: pre‑2000 models used the legendary OM601 and OM602 indirect‑injection diesels, while the 2000 facelift introduced common‑rail CDI technology.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes‑Benz T1N ladder frame • Predecessor: Mercedes‑Benz T1 • Successor: NCV3 (906 series) • Sibling body styles in this generation: Panel van, crew cab, chassis cab, minibus, pickup.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: No W901–905‑specific enquiry figure is available; the first‑generation Sprinter remains a commercial‑vehicle icon, with the OM601 and OM602 diesels generating the strongest enthusiast following.

items.quote: The 2.3‑litre naturally‑aspirated diesel — 79 PS, legendary durability, and a reputation for exceeding 300,000 miles with basic maintenance. The ultimate 'fit and forget' workhorse.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who value simplicity and durability above all else.

items.quote: The 2.9‑litre five‑cylinder turbo‑diesel — 122 PS, strong torque for heavier payloads, and the best balance of power and durability in the pre‑CDI range. The turbo is the only real weak point.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who need more power than the OM601 but want proven durability.

items.quote: The common‑rail CDI units brought common‑rail refinement but introduced electronic complexity and injector wear. High‑mileage examples without documented injector history should be approached with caution.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who can verify injector service history.

items.quote: The 2.7‑litre five‑cylinder CDI — 156 PS, the most powerful first‑gen Sprinter. But injector failure and turbo wear add up fast. The highest running costs of the T1N generation.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who need maximum power and are prepared for higher running costs.

items.quote: The 2.9‑litre five‑cylinder turbo‑diesel — 102 PS, proven durability, and moderate running costs. The sweet spot of pre‑CDI Sprinter ownership.
items.whoItsFor: the default choice for budget‑conscious Sprinter buyers who need a reliable workhorse.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Workhorse — strong torque, turbo is the only weak point
Best Diesel — more power, same durability
Modern Diesel — refined, but injectors are costly
Top CDI — most powerful, highest running costs

economicsRule.title: The Sprinter W901–905 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,500–£5,200 OM611 recon against a £2,000–£8,000 Sprinter sits at 31–260% of the van's value. On a sub‑£3,000 example, that brushes our 70% Mercedes threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well‑kept £6,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 42–87% — still worth considering if the van is otherwise sound. The OM601 is the exception — its low recon cost and legendary durability often make replacement viable even on lower‑value vans.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W901–905 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM601 2.3 diesel — cheapest recon, simplest maintenance.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W901–905 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM612 2.7 CDI — injectors and turbo work add up fast.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W901–905 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM602 2.9 TD (122 PS) — strong torque, proven durability, moderate running costs.

problems.title: OM601 / OM602 Injection Pump Wear
problems.rootCause: The Bosch mechanical injection pump on OM601 and OM602 engines can develop wear over time, leading to hard starting, poor running, and reduced power. The pumps are rebuildable but specialist work is required.
problems.recommendation: If your Phase I Sprinter develops hard starting or rough running, have the injection pump inspected immediately — a £400–£800 overhaul is cheaper than the £1,500+ replacement that follows a catastrophic failure.

cta.label: Compare OM601 / OM602 engine prices for your Sprinter →

problems.title: OM611 / OM612 Injector Failure
problems.affectedModels: W901–905 208 CDI, 308 CDI, 408 CDI, 211 CDI, 311 CDI, 411 CDI, 216 CDI, 316 CDI, 416 CDI (Phase II CDI diesels).
problems.rootCause: The common‑rail injectors on OM611 and OM612 engines can fail, leading to rough running, excessive smoke, and poor economy. Injector seals can also leak, causing "black death" — carbon buildup around the injector.
problems.recommendation: If your CDI Sprinter develops a rough idle or smoke, have the injectors tested immediately — a £400–£1,000 repair is far cheaper than the £2,500+ replacement that follows a full failure.

cta.label: Compare OM611 / OM612 engine prices for your Sprinter →

problems.title: OM602 Turbocharger Wear
problems.rootCause: The turbocharger on OM602 engines can develop wear over time, leading to reduced boost, smoke, and eventual failure. Oil supply issues are a common contributor.
problems.recommendation: If your OM602‑powered Sprinter develops a loss of power or excessive smoke, have the turbo inspected — a £400–£800 repair is cheaper than the £2,000+ replacement that follows a catastrophic failure.

cta.label: Compare OM602 engine prices for your Sprinter →

problems.title: M111 Timing Chain Stretch
problems.rootCause: The timing chain on M111 petrol engines can stretch over time, leading to rattling on start‑up and potential valve‑to‑piston contact if ignored.
problems.recommendation: If your petrol Sprinter develops a rattle on start‑up, have the timing chain inspected immediately — a £600–£1,200 repair is far cheaper than the £1,800+ replacement that follows a failure.

cta.label: Compare M111 engine prices for your Sprinter →
